title: Dog Leash Hanger
summary: A single-board wall hanger with stenciled lettering and a row of cup hooks, sized to your own collection of leashes and collars.
description:
This is a one-board project — a single cedar fence picket, cut to whatever length fits your wall and your number of leashes, stained, lettered, and hung with cup hooks. Sizing it around what you actually own rather than a fixed number is the right call here: count your leashes and collars first, then cut the board to fit them with a little breathing room, rather than guessing.

A stencil (cut on a vinyl cutter or hand-traced) gives cleaner lettering than freehand if you're not confident in your hand lettering, but freehand has real charm on a rustic piece like this. Finishing with an exterior-rated spar urethane rather than a standard interior poly is worth the extra coat if this hangs anywhere near a mudroom door or entryway that sees moisture.

materials.name: Stencil material or freehand design
materials.note: Cut on a vinyl cutter, hand-traced, or freehand.

cutList.note: 16" is a starting size — cut to fit your own number of leashes and your wording.

steps.title: Size and cut the board
steps.body: Count the leashes, collars and brushes this has to carry and lay them out side by side, then add room for whatever wording you want to paint on. The cut list's starting size is a single hanger board 16" long, and a cedar fence picket is about 5/8" thick and 5 1/2" wide, so that dimension is the only one you actually choose — the width and thickness come with the board. Cut the picket square to your chosen length; a 6 ft picket is 72" long, so one board covers any single-board length you settle on with room to spare. Cut off the picket's dog-eared point if your board includes it, unless you want to keep that shape as the top of the hanger.

steps.title: Sand smooth
steps.body: A fence picket comes rough-sawn, and stain sinks into torn fibers far darker than into sanded wood, so this is what decides whether the color lands evenly. Work the face, both long edges and both ends with the sander, starting coarse enough to knock the mill fuzz off and finishing fine. Ease the four corners and the arrises slightly so nothing catches a hand reaching for a leash. Vacuum or wipe the dust off before any stain touches it.

steps.title: Apply the stain
steps.body: Wipe stain onto the front face, both long edges and both ends, and leave the back bare if the board will sit flat against the wall — an unstained back costs nothing and saves a coat. Work it in with a rag or brush and wipe the excess back off before it gets tacky; cedar is soft and blotches if stain is left to pool. Let it dry fully before you lay any lettering over it, or the paint will lift the stain.

steps.title: Add the lettering
steps.body: Design and cut a stencil for your lettering and any graphic (a paw print, for example), or go freehand if you're comfortable with it. Paint the design onto the board.

steps.title: Install the hooks and hangers
steps.body: Mark the hook positions along a single line so they hang level, spacing the six cup hooks evenly — on the 16" starting length, six hooks 2 1/2" apart span 12 1/2" and leave about 1 3/4" clear at each end. Predrill every hook location with a bit slightly smaller than the hook's screw shank; cedar is soft enough that an undrilled hook will crush its own threads or split the board. Thread the cup hooks in by hand until they seat, turning them all so the openings face the same way. On the back, nail one sawtooth picture hanger near each end rather than a single center hanger — two spread points is what keeps a loaded board from tipping or pivoting on the wall.

steps.title: Apply the final finish
steps.body: Brush exterior spar urethane over the whole piece — face, edges, ends and around the lettering — before it goes on the wall, so you can reach every surface and lay the board flat while each coat levels. Spar urethane rather than an interior poly is the right call for anything near an entry door, where wet leashes and outside air do the damage. Apply three coats, letting each dry fully; no sanding between coats is needed. If a cup hook is in the way, back it out, finish under it, and thread it back into its existing hole.

steps.title: Hang it up
steps.body: Measure the exact distance between the two sawtooth hangers on the back and transfer that spacing to the wall, marking both points off one level line rather than measuring each down from the ceiling. Drive wall anchors or screws sized for a loaded board — several leashes and collars is more weight than a picture — and hook the board on. Check it with a level and adjust before you load it up.

images.alt: Wooden dog leash hanger with stenciled lettering and cup hooks
